CBAM Definitive Phase is live from 1 Jan 2026. First annual declaration due 30 September 2027. Penalty: €100 per tonne CO₂ not covered.

EU Regulation 2023/956 · Definitive Phase from 1 Jan 2026

CBAM — EU's Carbon Border
Adjustment Mechanism

From 1 January 2026, every tonne of steel, aluminium, cement, fertiliser, hydrogen or electricity exported to the EU carries a verified carbon price. Without a compliant emissions calculation and declared CBAM certificates, your EU importer faces €100/tCO₂ penalties — with no cap.

Understanding CBAM

What is CBAM and
why does it matter now?

The Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), established by Regulation (EU) 2023/956, places a carbon price on imports of certain carbon-intensive goods into the EU. It ensures imported goods face the same carbon cost as EU-produced goods under the EU Emissions Trading System (ETS) — preventing carbon leakage where production relocates to avoid EU climate costs.

After a 27-month transitional reporting phase, CBAM entered its definitive (enforcement) phase on 1 January 2026. Your EU importer must now purchase and surrender CBAM certificates equal to the embedded emissions in goods imported from you. The certificate price is linked to the weekly average EU ETS carbon price.

The correct CBAM cost formula per Regulation (EU) 2023/956 and implementing acts is:
CBAM cost = Volume × Embedded EF × ETS price × CBAM phase-in factor
where the CBAM factor reflects the phase-out of ETS free allowances: 2.5% in 2026, 5% in 2027, 10% in 2028 … 100% in 2034.

Without your verified emissions data, your EU importer must use country-specific default values set by the Commission (Implementing Regulation EU 2025/2621), which carry an added punitive markup: +10% in 2026, +20% in 2027, +30% from 2028 (except fertilisers: +1% all years per EC decision Dec 2025).

€100/tCO₂ penalty — uncapped

Non-compliance in the definitive phase: €100 per tonne of CO₂ not covered by surrendered certificates. The obligation to surrender the missing certificates remains even after paying the penalty.

Certificate sale opens 1 February 2027

CBAM certificates for 2026 imports are sold via the common central platform from 1 February 2027. The first surrender deadline is 30 September 2027, covering all goods imported into the EU in calendar year 2026.

Third-party verification mandatory

All declared emissions must be verified by an EU-accredited third-party verifier (ISO 14065). Year 1 requires an on-site inspection. Default values carry a punitive markup; verified data eliminates it and dramatically lowers certificate costs.

Verified data = lower cost

Only an explicit carbon price effectively paid in the production country (a national ETS or carbon tax) can be deducted from CBAM obligations. Voluntary carbon credits do not count under Regulation (EU) 2023/956.

Official CBAM cost formula — Regulation (EU) 2023/956 & Implementing Acts
CBAM Certificates (tCO₂e) = Volume (t) × Embedded EF (tCO₂e/t) × CBAM Phase-in Factor
CBAM Certificate Cost (€) = CBAM Certificates (tCO₂e) × ETS Price (€/tCO₂e)
Embedded Emission Factor (EF)
Either verified actual installation-level data (preferred) or EU-assigned country-specific default values per Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2621. Default values include a punitive markup: +10% (2026), +20% (2027), +30% from 2028. Fertilisers: +1% all years.
CBAM Phase-in Factor
Reflects the progressive phase-out of ETS free allowances: 2.5% (2026), 5% (2027), 10% (2028), 16% (2029), 22% (2030), 34% (2031), 49% (2032), 70% (2033), 100% (2034). Source: CBAM Reg. Art. 22 & Annex IV.
ETS Price
CBAM certificate price = weekly average ETS auction price from the previous week (Art. 21, Reg. 2023/956, as amended by Reg. 2025/2083). In 2026: quarterly average auction price is used. From 2027: weekly average. Not a fixed value — fluctuates with the carbon market.
Sources: Regulation (EU) 2023/956 (CBAM Regulation, as amended by (EU) 2025/2083) · Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2621 (default values) · Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2620 (free allocation adjustment) · Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2548 (certificate price calculation)
Covered sectors & official default values

Six sectors in scope. World-average default emission values
from EU Commission JRC (2023), used as reference baseline.

Default values below are the transitional period world-average values published by the European Commission (22 December 2023) from the JRC study (JRC134682). For 2026+, country-specific default values apply per IR (EU) 2025/2621 with the punitive markup noted. These are the only officially published reference values available.

Iron & Steel
CN Chapter 72 (most headings) + 73xx + 2601 12
Pig iron (7201)2.07 tCO₂e/t
Steel semi-finished (7207, rolled)2.21 tCO₂e/t
HRC ≥600mm wide (7208)2.28 tCO₂e/t
CRC ≥600mm (7209)2.39 tCO₂e/t
Rebar / wire rods (7213)2.21 tCO₂e/t
Stainless steel (7218/7219)4.08–4.61 tCO₂e/t
Structures (7308)5.01 tCO₂e/t
Screws/bolts (7318)2.21 tCO₂e/t
Aluminium
CN 7601–7616
Unwrought aluminium (7601)~1.49 tCO₂e/t (direct)
Aluminium bars & rods (7604)~1.49 tCO₂e/t
Aluminium wire (7605)~1.49 tCO₂e/t
Aluminium plates/sheets (7606)~1.49 tCO₂e/t
Aluminium foil (7607)~1.49 tCO₂e/t
Aluminium tubes (7608)~1.49 tCO₂e/t
Aluminium structures (7610)~1.49 tCO₂e/t
Other aluminium articles (7616)~1.49 tCO₂e/t
Cement
CN 2507 00 80 + 2523 10–90
Kaolinic clays (2507 00 80)~0.05 tCO₂e/t
Cement clinker (2523 10 00)~0.83 tCO₂e/t (direct)
White Portland cement (2523 21)~0.76 tCO₂e/t (direct)
Other Portland cement (2523 29)~0.76 tCO₂e/t (direct)
Aluminous cement (2523 30)~0.76 tCO₂e/t
Other hydraulic cements (2523 90)~0.76 tCO₂e/t
Fertilisers
CN 2808, 2814, 2834 21, 3102, 3105 (except 3105 60)
Nitric acid (2808 00 00)~1.33 tCO₂e/t (direct)
Ammonia (2814)~1.69 tCO₂e/t (direct)
Potassium nitrate (2834 21)~0.73 tCO₂e/t
Urea (3102 10)~1.94 tCO₂e/t (direct)
Nitrogenous fertilisers (3102)~1.69 tCO₂e/t (avg)
Mixed NPK fertilisers (3105)~0.73–1.69 tCO₂e/t
Hydrogen
CN 2804 10 00
Hydrogen (2804 10 00)~9.66 tCO₂e/t H₂ (world avg)
Grey hydrogen (SMR, natural gas)~10+ tCO₂e/t H₂
Blue hydrogen (SMR + CCS)~2–3 tCO₂e/t H₂
Green hydrogen (electrolysis, renew.)~0.5–1 tCO₂e/t H₂

Note: hydrogen is not subject to the 50t de minimis exemption. Source: JRC135067 (Dolci & Arrigoni, 2023).

Electricity
CN 2716 00 00
Electrical energy (2716 00 00)Country-specific grid EF
Default: fossil fuel-based EFPer production country

Electricity is excluded from the 50t de minimis rule. Default values for electricity are based on the country's fossil fuel-based power generation intensity, not a global average. Direct electricity trade from India to EU is currently negligible — electricity is most relevant as an indirect emission source for aluminium, cement and fertiliser.

CBAM regulatory timeline

From reporting to financial enforcement.

2023–25
Transitional phase

Quarterly emissions reporting only (Oct 2023 – Dec 2025)

No financial certificates required

World-average default values allowed (no markup)

Last quarterly report: 31 Jan 2026

2026
NOW — Definitive phase

Financial obligations begin 1 Jan 2026

Annual declarations replace quarterly reports

Third-party verification mandatory (on-site yr 1)

Declarant application deadline: 31 March 2026

CBAM factor: 2.5%

Default value markup: +10% (steel/Al/cement), +1% (fertiliser)

2027
Financial enforcement

Certificate sales open: 1 February 2027

First annual declaration & surrender: 30 Sept 2027 (2026 imports)

CBAM factor: 5%

Default markup: +20% (steel/Al/cement), +1% (fertiliser)

ETS price = weekly average (not quarterly)

2028–34
Full enforcement

~180 downstream products added from 2028

Default markup: +30% from 2028 onward

CBAM factor rises annually to 100% in 2034

ETS free allowances fully phased out by 2034

ETS price projected ~€130/t by 2030

What you must do

CBAM compliance action plan for 2026

Six steps every Indian exporter of CBAM goods to the EU must complete. SustainCore manages all of them end to end.

Step 1 · Urgent
01
Confirm CBAM scope & CN codes
Verify your products fall under Annex I of Regulation (EU) 2023/956 using exact 8-digit CN codes. Confirm your EU importer's annual volume against the 50-tonne threshold. Check all product lines including precursors.
  • Map all exported products to Annex I CN codes
  • Confirm annual volume vs 50t de minimis threshold
  • Identify all in-scope precursors in your value chain
  • Brief your EU importer on their declarant obligations
Step 2 · Critical
02
Calculate embedded emissions per EU method
Your EU importer needs verified, installation-level embedded emissions data per tonne per product in the EU Communication Template format. Without it, they pay punitive default values with +10% markup in 2026.
  • Production-level GHG inventory per installation
  • Calculate direct emissions (and indirect where required)
  • Use EU-prescribed methodology per Annex IV of IR 2023/1773
  • Prepare EU Communication Template data per shipment
Step 3 · Required
03
Third-party verification (ISO 14065)
From 2026, all CBAM emissions data must be verified by an EU-accredited third-party verifier. Year 1 requires mandatory on-site audit at your production facility. Failed verification reverts to default values.
  • Select EU-accredited ISO 14065 verifier
  • Prepare for mandatory on-site inspection (2026)
  • Ensure monitoring meets EU methodology requirements
  • Data must cover the full 2026 calendar year
Step 4 · Ongoing
04
Support your EU importer's declaration
CBAM obligations rest legally with the EU importer — but they depend entirely on data from you. Your ability to deliver clean verified emissions data becomes a direct commercial differentiator in procurement decisions.
  • Provide verified data in EU Communication Template format
  • Establish annual data delivery calendar aligned to deadlines
  • Document all emissions data with full audit trail
  • Coordinate directly with EU importer's compliance team
Step 5 · 2026
05
Claim carbon price deductions (if applicable)
Under Art. 9 of Regulation (EU) 2023/956, any explicit carbon price effectively paid in the country of production (a national ETS or carbon tax — not voluntary credits) can be deducted from CBAM certificate obligations, subject to verified documentation.
  • Identify eligible India carbon pricing mechanisms (CCTS, PAT)
  • Calculate deductible amount per tonne of CO₂
  • Obtain independent verification of carbon price paid
  • Submit deduction documentation via EU importer's declaration
Step 6 · Strategic
06
Decarbonise to reduce certificate costs
CBAM creates a direct financial incentive to reduce emissions. Every tCO₂ eliminated saves you the ETS price (currently €60–80/t, rising to €130 by 2030). A decarbonisation roadmap protects long-term EU market competitiveness.
  • Map high-impact emission reduction opportunities
  • Model ROI: decarbonisation investment vs CBAM savings
  • Align with SBTi 1.5°C near-term pathway
  • Build a phased investment and reduction roadmap
CBAM Cost Estimator

Calculate your CBAM exposure

Estimate annual CBAM certificate costs using the official formula: Volume × Embedded Emission Factor × CBAM Phase-in Factor × ETS Price. All default emission values are from the EU Commission's official publications.

Calculator formula (per Regulation EU 2023/956):
CBAM Certificates (tCO₂e) = Volume (t) × Embedded EF (tCO₂e/t) × CBAM Phase-in Factor  |  Cost (€) = Certificates × ETS Price (€/tCO₂e)
Default values include punitive markup: +10% (2026), +20% (2027), +30% (2028+). Exception: Fertilisers +1% all years per EU Commission Dec 2025 decision. Voluntary carbon credits cannot be deducted — only explicit carbon taxes/ETS effectively paid in country of production.
€40€150
€70/t
Carbon price paid in production country (Art. 9 deduction)
Only explicit national ETS or carbon tax. Voluntary carbon credits are NOT deductible under Regulation (EU) 2023/956 Art. 9.
Default values sourced from EU Commission JRC study (JRC134682). For 2026, markup of +10% applied for steel/aluminium/cement, +1% for fertilisers (per EC Dec 2025 decision). ETS price is indicative.
CBAM cost — using default values (incl. markup)
€ —
Estimated annual certificate cost for your EU importer
Export volume—
Default EF (incl. markup)—
Actual EF (verified data)—
ETS price—
CBAM phase-in factor—
CBAM certificates (default)—
CBAM certificates (actual)—
Cost with actual data—
Annual saving from verified data—
Formula: Volume × EF × CBAM factor × ETS price. Estimates for planning only. Actual costs depend on country-specific 2026 default values per IR (EU) 2025/2621, official weekly ETS auction averages, and verified installation-level data. Consult SustainCore for a certified CBAM assessment.
€40€150
€70/t
Product / CN CodeVolume (t)Default EFActual EFCerts (default)Cost (default)Cost (actual)Saving
€—
Total cost (default values)
€—
Total cost (actual data)
€—
Total annual saving
Default emission factors from EU Commission JRC study (JRC134682, Dec 2023). Markup applied: +10% (2026), +20% (2027), +30% (2028+) for steel/Al/cement; +1% all years for fertilisers. Enter your verified actual EF to see cost reduction. Formula: Volume × EF × CBAM factor × ETS price.
YearCBAM FactorEst. ETS priceDefault markupCertificates (tCO₂e)Est. CBAM costPhase-in %
ETS price: grows ~5%/year from base as per analyst consensus projections. CBAM factors per Article 22 and Annex IV, Regulation (EU) 2023/956 (as amended). Default markup: +10% (2026), +20% (2027), +30% (2028+) per Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2621. Phase-in factor reaches 100% in 2034, representing full phase-out of free ETS allowances in CBAM sectors. This projection uses your actual EF (no markup applied). For planning purposes only.
Why verified actual data matters — a concrete example
10,000 tonnes of hot-rolled steel (7208) exported to the EU. ETS price €70/t. Year 2026 (CBAM factor 2.5%).
Using EU default values (no verified data)
Default EF (7208, world avg)2.28 tCO₂/t
+10% punitive markup (2026)2.508 tCO₂/t
Total embedded emissions25,080 tCO₂
CBAM certificates (× 2.5%)627 tCO₂e
Cost at €70/t (2026)€43,890
Cost at €130/t (2034, 100%)€3,260,400
Using verified actual installation data
Actual EF (efficient plant)1.60 tCO₂/t
No punitive markup1.60 tCO₂/t
Total embedded emissions16,000 tCO₂
CBAM certificates (× 2.5%)400 tCO₂e
Cost at €70/t (2026)€28,000
Cost at €130/t (2034, 100%)€2,080,000
Saving from verified data: €15,890 in 2026 → €1,180,400 by 2034 for this single product shipment
Penalties for non-compliance — Definitive Phase
Per Article 26 and 27, Regulation (EU) 2023/956
€100/tCO₂
Penalty per tonne not covered by surrendered certificates. The obligation to surrender the missing certificates remains even after paying the penalty.
Import ban
Failure to obtain or maintain authorised declarant status results in the EU customs authority denying import of CBAM goods.
No cap
Transitional phase penalties were €10–50/t (capped). Definitive phase: €100/t with no cap. Exposure scales directly with volume and the phase-in factor.
SustainCore CBAM services

How SustainCore makes
CBAM compliance simple

End-to-end CBAM compliance for Indian and global exporters — from initial exposure assessment to ongoing verified emissions data delivery to your EU importers.

01
CBAM Scope Assessment & Exposure Quantification
Rapid, accurate assessment confirming which product lines fall in scope, confirming CN codes against Annex I, calculating your volume against the 50t threshold, and modelling certificate costs under both default and actual emission scenarios for 2026–2034.
  • CN code mapping against Regulation (EU) 2023/956 Annex I
  • Volume threshold confirmation and annual exposure modelling
  • Default vs actual cost comparison with savings quantification
  • Priority action plan ranked by financial impact
02
Embedded Emissions Calculation (EU Methodology)
We calculate embedded GHG emissions using the EU's prescribed methodology per Annex IV of Implementing Regulation (EU) 2023/1773 — not national equivalents. Covering direct (Scope 1) emissions and indirect electricity emissions where required (cement, fertilisers), formatted in the EU Communication Template your EU importer needs.
  • Production-level GHG inventory per installation (Annex IV methodology)
  • Direct emissions: combustion, process, fugitive
  • Indirect electricity emissions for cement and fertilisers
  • EU Communication Template data per shipment per product
03
Third-Party Verification Coordination
We prepare your data to verification standard and coordinate with an EU-accredited ISO 14065 verifier — including the mandatory on-site inspection in 2026. We pre-close all potential findings before the verifier arrives, preventing a failed verification that would revert to costly default values with markup.
  • Verification readiness assessment and evidence documentation
  • EU-accredited ISO 14065 verifier selection and coordination
  • On-site audit preparation and accompaniment
  • 5% materiality threshold compliance assurance
04
EU Importer Data Delivery System
We establish an annual data delivery system ensuring verified emissions data reaches your EU importer before their 30 September declaration deadline, in the exact EU Communication Template format required by the CBAM Registry.
  • Annual emissions data calendar aligned to EU deadlines
  • Per-shipment EU Communication Template preparation
  • Direct coordination with your EU importer's compliance team
  • Regulatory update monitoring for your specific products
05
Art. 9 Carbon Price Deduction Support
Under Article 9 of Regulation (EU) 2023/956, only explicit carbon prices effectively paid in the production country (national ETS or carbon tax — not voluntary credits) can be deducted. We identify eligible Indian mechanisms, calculate the deductible amount and prepare the verification documentation required for the deduction claim.
  • Identification of Art. 9–eligible India carbon pricing instruments
  • Deductible amount calculation per tonne CO₂
  • Independent verification of carbon price paid
  • Deduction documentation for EU importer's declaration
06
CBAM-Aligned Decarbonisation Roadmap
Every tonne eliminated from your production saves the ETS price (€70+/t now, ~€130/t by 2030) — multiplied by the rising CBAM factor. We build a production-level decarbonisation roadmap that identifies the highest-return emission reduction investments and models their ROI against projected certificate savings year by year.
  • Production-level emission reduction opportunity mapping
  • ROI modelling: investment vs cumulative CBAM savings
  • Fuel switching, process optimisation, energy efficiency options
  • SBTi 1.5°C near-term target alignment
Your CBAM journey with SustainCore

From first enquiry to fully compliant — in 6–8 weeks

A simple, structured process. You provide data. We handle the complexity. Your EU importer gets the verified emissions declarations they need.

Questions answered

CBAM FAQs

Per Regulation (EU) 2023/956 and its implementing acts: (1) CBAM Certificates (tCO₂e) = Volume (tonnes) × Embedded Emission Factor (tCO₂e/tonne) × CBAM Phase-in Factor. (2) Certificate cost (€) = CBAM Certificates × ETS weekly average auction price (€/tCO₂e). The embedded emission factor is either your verified actual installation data or the Commission's country-specific default values per Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2621. The CBAM phase-in factor reflects the phase-out of ETS free allowances: 2.5% in 2026, 5% in 2027, 10% in 2028, rising to 100% in 2034.

Your EU importer needs installation-level embedded emissions data in the EU Communication Template format. This includes: total embedded direct emissions per tonne of each CBAM product produced at your installation, indirect electricity emissions where required (cement, fertilisers), the production route classification, and evidence of any eligible carbon price paid in India (if applicable). Data must cover the full calendar year of imports (e.g., full 2026 for the September 2027 declaration). All data must be calculated using the EU method per Annex IV of Implementing Regulation (EU) 2023/1773 and verified by an accredited third party. Without this data, your EU importer defaults to country-specific default values — which include a punitive markup.

The country-specific default values for the 2026 definitive phase are established in Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/2621 (16 December 2025). These are different from the world-average values in the transitional period document (Dec 2023) — they are set at the average emission intensity of India for each product and production route, plus the punitive markup (+10% in 2026). The transitional-period world average values from JRC study JRC134682 serve as the publicly available reference baseline used in our calculator. For exact country-specific 2026 default values for your specific products and CN codes, SustainCore conducts a certified assessment using the official implementing regulation data.

No. Article 9 of Regulation (EU) 2023/956 is explicit: only an explicit carbon price effectively paid in the country of production — such as a national ETS or a carbon tax — can be deducted from CBAM certificate obligations. Voluntary carbon credits purchased by the supplier or the importer do not count. This is a hard legal rule, not an interpretation. India's emerging Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under the Energy Conservation Act, once fully operational with explicit per-tonne carbon price mechanisms, may create deductible costs in future — but this requires regulatory confirmation per the delegated acts on Art. 9 implementation expected in 2026.

The 50-tonne de minimis threshold per Article 2(3a) of Regulation (EU) 2025/2083 is assessed at the EU importer level — covering their total annual imports of cement, iron and steel, aluminium and fertilisers from all origins combined. If your EU importer purchases CBAM goods from multiple suppliers, their total may exceed 50 tonnes even if your individual portion is small. The threshold does not apply per-supplier or per-shipment. Hydrogen and electricity are excluded from the de minimis rule regardless of volume. Confirm your EU importer's total CBAM import volume to determine whether the threshold applies to them.

The European Commission CBAM Committee adopted a special treatment for fertilisers in December 2025 (confirmed in Implementing Regulation EU 2025/2621): while steel, aluminium and cement default values carry +10% markup in 2026, rising to +30% by 2028, fertiliser default values carry only a +1% markup across all years. This reflects the fertiliser industry's concern that high CBAM costs would be passed through to consumer food prices — an inflation and food security consideration that led the Commission to constrain the fertiliser markup. As a result, the incentive to report actual verified emissions data is proportionally smaller for fertilisers than for steel or aluminium.

The CBAM phase-in factor reflects the progressive phase-out of free ETS allowances. In 2026 at 2.5%, CBAM certificates represent only 2.5% of total embedded emissions × ETS price. By 2034 at 100%, the full embedded emissions carry the full ETS price. Simultaneously, the EU ETS price is projected to rise from ~€70 today to ~€130 by 2030 as allowances tighten. The combined effect: a 10,000-tonne steel exporter with 1.9 tCO₂/t faces ~€33,250 in CBAM costs in 2026 (2.5% × €70) versus ~€2.47 million in 2034 (100% × €130). This 74× increase in cost over 8 years is the strongest possible incentive to start reducing embedded emissions now.

CBAM is not optional.
Neither is acting now.

Every shipment to the EU from January 2026 creates a CBAM liability. Every day without verified emissions data increases your EU importer's default-value exposure and puts your contracts at risk. SustainCore delivers full CBAM compliance in 6–8 weeks.

Book free CBAM assessment → Use the calculator first
